topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification flashed across my screen, a familiar chime that usually signaled a new assignment or a class announcement. This time, it was a direct message from Mr. Harrison. My stomach clenched, a tight, cold knot forming beneath my ribs. I hesitated, my thumb hovering over the 'open' button. He had followed me. Not just followed, but actively engaged with my posts. Every picture I’d shared—a blurry shot of a sunset, a doodle in my notebook—was apparently being cataloged by him. I quickly minimized the application, the glow of the screen feeling suddenly harsh in the dim light of my room. I stared at the ceiling, the silence of the apartment amplifying the frantic thumping in my chest. It felt like a spotlight had been unexpectedly switched on, illuminating something I desperately wished remained hidden.

[story 2]

I kept glancing at my phone, the screen a beacon of unwelcome attention. It was impossible to focus on the history reading; my mind kept drifting back to the recent activity on my profile. Professor Davies, the man who lectured with such measured calm, was there. He’d liked a post about a forgotten historical figure. A small, insignificant interaction, yet it felt monumental, a breach of the professional boundary I’d always assumed existed between us. I picked up my pen, but the ink blurred on the page. My hands began to shake slightly, a subtle tremor that made holding the pen feel clumsy and awkward. I shoved the phone into my bag, the weight of it heavy, and walked to the window, watching the street below, trying to steady the erratic rhythm of my breathing.

[story 3]

The realization hit me like a physical blow while I was scrolling through my feed. Mr. Evans, my calculus instructor, had been viewing my stories. I felt a sudden, intense heat rise up my neck, prickling my skin. It wasn't just the observation; it was the implication—that he was watching my life outside the classroom, parsing the fragments I offered up online. I stood frozen in the hallway, clutching my backpack straps so tightly my knuckles were white. Every sound in the corridor seemed magnified—the squeak of a shoe, the distant murmur of voices. A deep sense of unease settled over me, a heavy cloak of apprehension that made the air feel thin in my lungs. I needed to get out, needed distance, needed to erase the evidence of this unwelcome scrutiny.
